text: The words of Ecclesiastes, the son of David, king of Jerusalem.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Vanity of vanities, said Ecclesiastes: vanity of vanities, and all is vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What hath a man more of all his labour, that he taketh under the sun?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: One generation passeth away, and another generation cometh: but the earth standeth for ever.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The sun riseth, and goeth down, and returneth to his place: and there rising again,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Maketh his round by the south, and turneth again to the north: the spirit goeth forward surveying all places round about, and returneth to his circuits.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the rivers run into the sea, yet the sea doth not overflow: unto the place from whence the rivers come, they return, to flow again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All things are hard: man cannot explain them by word. The eye is not filled with seeing, neither is the ear filled with hearing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What is it that hath been? the same thing that shall be. What is it that hath been done? the same that shall be done.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Nothing under the sun is new, neither is any man able to say: Behold this is new: for it hath already gone before in the ages that were before us.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is no remembrance of former things: nor indeed of those things which hereafter are to come, shall there be any remembrance with them that shall be in the latter end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I Ecclesiastes was king over Israel in Jerusalem,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I proposed in my mind to seek and search out wisely concerning all things that are done under the sun. This painful occupation hath God given to the children of men, to be exercised therein.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have seen all things that are done under the sun, and behold all is vanity, and vexation of spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The perverse are hard to be corrected, and the number of fools is infinite.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have spoken in my heart, saying: Behold I am become great, and have gone beyond all in wisdom, that were before me in Jerusalem: and my mind hath contemplated many things wisely, and I have learned.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I have given my heart to know prudence, and learning, and errors, and folly: and I have perceived that in these also there was labour, and vexation of spirit,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because in much wisdom there is much indignation: and he that addeth knowledge, addeth also labour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I said in my heart: I will go, and abound with delights, and enjoy good things. And I saw that this also was vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Laughter I counted error: and to mirth I said: Why art thou vainly deceived?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I thought in my heart, to withdraw my flesh from wine, that I might turn my mind to wisdom, and might avoid folly, till I might see what was profitable for the children of men: and what they ought to do under the sun, all the days of their life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I made me great works, I built me houses, and planted vineyards,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I made gardens, and orchards, and set them with trees of all kinds,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I made me ponds of water, to water therewith the wood of the young trees,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I got me menservants, and maidservants, and had a great family: and herds of oxen, and great flocks of sheep, above all that were before me in Jerusalem:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I heaped together for myself silver and gold, and the wealth of kings, and provinces: I made me singing men, and singing women, and the delights of the sons of men, cups and vessels to serve to pour out wine:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I surpassed in riches all that were before me in Jerusalem: my wisdom also remained with me.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whatsoever my eyes desired, I refused them not: and I withheld not my heart from enjoying every pleasure, and delighting itself in the things which I had prepared: and esteemed this my portion, to make use of my own labour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And when I turned myself to all the works which my hands had wrought, and to the labours wherein I had laboured in vain, I saw in all things vanity, and vexation of mind, and that nothing was lasting under the sun.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I passed further to behold wisdom, and errors and folly, (What is man, said I that he can follow the King his maker?)

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I saw that wisdom excelled folly, as much as light differeth from darkness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The eyes of a wise man are in his head: the fool walketh in darkness: and I learned that they were to die both alike.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I said in my heart: If the death of the fool and mine shall be one, what doth it avail me, that I have applied myself more to the study of wisdom? And speaking with my own mind, I perceived that this also was vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there shall be no remembrance of the wise no more than of the fool forever, and the times to come shall cover all things together with oblivion: the learned dieth in like manner as the unlearned.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And therefore I was weary of my life, when I saw that all things under the sun are evil, and all vanity and vexation of spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Again I hated all my application wherewith I had earnestly laboured under the sun, being like to have an heir after me,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whom I know not whether he will be a wise man or a fool, and he shall have rule over all my labours with which I have laboured and been solicitous: and is there anything so vain?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wherefore I left off and my heart renounced labouring anymore under the sun.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For when a man laboureth in wisdom, and knowledge, and carefulness, he leaveth what he hath gotten to an idle man: so this also is vanity, and a great evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For what profit shall a man have of all his labour, and vexation of spirit, with which he hath been tormented under the sun?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All his days are full of sorrows and miseries, even in the night he doth not rest in mind: and is not this vanity?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Is it not better to eat and drink, and to shew his soul good things of his labours? and this is from the hand of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who shall so feast and abound with delights as I?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: God hath given to a man that is good in his sight, wisdom, and knowledge, and joy: but to the sinner he hath given vexation, and superfluous care, to heap up and to gather together, and to give it to him that hath pleased God: but this also is vanity, and a fruitless solicitude of the mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All things have their season, and in their times all things pass under heaven.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A time to be born and a time to die. A time to plant, and a time to pluck up that which is planted.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A time to kill, and a time to heal. A time to destroy, and a time to build.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A time to weep, and a time to laugh. A time to mourn, and a time to dance.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A time to scatter stones, and a time to gather. A time to embrace, and a time to be far from embraces.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A time to get, and a time to lose. A time to keep, and a time to cast away.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A time to rend, and a time to sew. A time to keep silence, and a time to speak.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A time of love, and a time of hatred. A time of war, and a time of peace.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What hath man more of his labour?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have seen the trouble, which God hath given the sons of men to be exercised in it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath made all things good in their time, and hath delivered the world to their consideration, so that man cannot find out the work which God hath made from the beginning to the end.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I have known that there was no better thing than to rejoice, and to do well in this life.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For every man that eateth and drinketh, and seeth good of his labour, this is the gift of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have learned that all the works which God hath made, continue for ever: we cannot add any thing, nor take away from those things which God hath made that he may be feared.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: That which hath been made, the same continueth: the things that shall be, have already been: and God restoreth that which is past.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I saw under the sun in the place of judgment wickedness, and in the place of justice iniquity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I said in my heart: God shall judge both the just and the wicked, and then shall be the time of every thing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I said in my heart concerning the sons of men, that God would prove them, and shew them to be like beasts.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore the death of man, and of beasts is one, and the condition of them both is equal: as man dieth, so they also die: all things breathe alike, and man hath nothing more than beast: all things are subject to vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all things go to one place: of earth they were made, and into earth they return together.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Who knoweth if the spirit of the children of Adam ascend upward, and if the spirit of the beasts descend downward?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I have found that nothing is better than for a man to rejoice in his work, and that this is his portion. For who shall bring him to know the things that shall be after him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I turned myself to other things, and I saw the oppressions that are done under the sun, and the tears of the innocent, and they had no comforter; and they were not able to resist their violence, being destitute of help from any.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I praised the dead rather than the living:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I judged him happier than them both, that is not yet born, nor hath seen the evils that are done under the sun.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Again I considered all the labours of men, and I remarked that their industries are exposed to the envy of their neighbour: so in this also there is vanity, and fruitless care.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The fool foldeth his hands together, and eateth his own flesh, saying:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is a handful with rest, than both hands full with labour, and vexation of mind.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Considering I found also another vanity under the sun:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is but one, and he hath not a second, no child, no brother, and yet he ceaseth not to labour, neither are his eyes satisfied with riches, neither doth he reflect, saying: For whom do I labour, and defraud my soul of good things? in this also is vanity, and a grievous vexation.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better therefore that two should be together, than one: for they have the advantage of their society:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If one fall he shall be supported by the other: woe to him that is alone, for when he falleth, he hath none to lift him up.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if two lie together, they shall warm one another: how shall one alone be warmed?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And if a man prevail against one, two shall withstand him: a threefold cord is not easily broken.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is a child that is poor and wise, than a king that is old and foolish, who knoweth not to foresee for hereafter.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because out of prison and chains sometimes a man cometh forth to a kingdom: and another born king is consumed with poverty.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I saw all men living, that walk under the sun with the second young man, who shall rise up in his place.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The number of the people, of all that were before him is infinite: and they that shall come afterwards, shall not rejoice in him: but this also is vanity, and vexation of spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Keep thy foot, when thou goest into the house of God, and draw nigh to hear. For much better is obedience, than the victims of fools, who know not what evil they do.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Speak not any thing rashly, and let not thy heart be hasty to utter a word before God. For God is in heaven, and thou upon earth: therefore let thy words be few.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Dreams follow many cares: and in many words shall be found folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou hast vowed any thing to God, defer not to pay it: for an unfaithful and foolish promise displeaseth him: but whatsoever thou hast vowed, pay it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And it is much better not to vow, than after a vow not to perform the things promised.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give not thy mouth to cause thy flesh to sin: and say not before the angel: There is no providence: lest God be angry at thy words, and destroy all the works of thy hands.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where there are many dreams, there are many vanities, and words without number: but do thou fear God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If thou shalt see the oppressions of the poor, and violent judgments, and justice perverted in the province, wonder not at this matter: for he that is high hath another higher, and there are others still higher than these:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Moreover there is the king that reigneth over all the land subject to him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A covetous man shall not be satisfied with money: and he that loveth riches shall reap no fruit from them: so this also is vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Where there are great riches, there are also many to eat them. And what doth it profit the owner, but that he seeth the riches with his eyes?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Sleep is sweet to a labouring man, whether he eat little or much: but the fulness of the rich will not suffer him to sleep.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is also another grievous evil, which I have seen under the sun: riches kept to the hurt of the owner.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For they are lost with very great affliction: he hath begotten a son, who shall be in extremity of want.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As he came forth naked from his mother’s womb, so shall he return, and shall take nothing away with him of his labour.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A most deplorable evil: as he came, so shall he return. What then doth it profit him that he hath laboured for the wind?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the days of his life he eateth in darkness, and in many cares, and in misery, and sorrow.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This therefore hath seemed good to me, that a man should eat and drink, and enjoy the fruit of his labour, wherewith he hath laboured under the sun, all the days of his life, which God hath given him: and this is his portion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And every man to whom God hath given riches, and substance, and hath given him power to eat thereof, and to enjoy his portion, and to rejoice of his labour: this is the gift of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he shall not much remember the days of his life, because God entertaineth his heart with delight.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is also another evil, which I have seen under the sun, and that frequent among men:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A man to whom God hath given riches, and substance, and honour, and his soul wanteth nothing of all that he desireth: yet God doth not give him power to eat thereof, but a stranger shall eat it up. This is vanity and a great misery.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If a man beget a hundred children, and live many years, and attain to a great age, and his soul make no use of the goods of his substance, and he be without burial: of this man I pronounce, that the untimely born is better than he.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For he came in vain, and goeth to darkness, and his name shall be wholly forgotten.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He hath not seen the sun, nor known the distance of good and evil:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Although he lived two thousand years, and hath not enjoyed good things: do not all make haste to one place?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All the labour of man is for his mouth, but his soul shall not be filled.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What hath the wise man more than the fool? and what the poor man, but to go thither, where there is life?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better it is to see what thou mayst desire, than to desire that which thou canst not know. But this also is vanity, and presumption of spirit.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that shall be, his name is already called: and it is known, that he is a man, and cannot contend in judgment with him that is stronger than himself.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There are many words that have much vanity in disputing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: What needeth a man to seek things that are above him, whereas he knoweth not what is profitable for him in his life, in all the days of his pilgrimage, and the time that passeth like a shadow? Or who can tell him what shall be after him under the sun?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A good name is better than precious ointments: and the day of death than the day of one’s birth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better to go to the house of mourning, than to the house of feasting: for in that we are put in mind of the end of all, and the living thinketh what is to come.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Anger is better than laughter: because by the sadness of the countenance the mind of the offender is corrected.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of the wise is where there is mourning, and the heart of fools where there is mirth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is better to be rebuked by a wise man, than to be deceived by the flattery of fools.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as the crackling of thorns burning under a pot, so is the laughter of a fool: now this also is vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Oppression troubleth the wise, and shall destroy the strength of his heart.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is the end of a speech than the beginning. Better is the patient man than the presumptuous.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not quickly angry: for anger resteth in the bosom of a fool.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Say not: What thinkest thou is the cause that former times were better than they are now? for this manner of question is foolish.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom with riches is more profitable, and bringeth more advantage to them that see the sun.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For as wisdom is a defence, so money is a defence: but learning and wisdom excel in this, that they give life to him that possesseth them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Consider the works of God, that no man can correct whom he hath despised.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the good day enjoy good things, and beware beforehand of the evil day: for God hath made both the one and the other, that man may not find against him any just complaint.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: These things also I saw in the days of my vanity: A just man perisheth in his justice, and a wicked man liveth a long time in his wickedness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not over just: and be not more wise than is necessary, lest thou become stupid.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not overmuch wicked: and be not foolish, lest thou die before thy time.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is good that thou shouldst hold up the just, yea and from him withdraw not thy hand: for he that feareth God, neglecteth nothing.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Wisdom hath strengthened the wise more than ten princes of the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For there is no just man upon earth, that doth good, and sinneth not.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But do not apply thy heart to all words that are spoken: lest perhaps thou hear thy servant reviling thee.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For thy conscience knoweth that thou also hast often spoken evil of others.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have tried all things in wisdom. I have said: I will be wise: and it departed farther from me,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Much more than it was: it is a great depth, who shall find it out?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have surveyed all things with my mind, to know, and consider, and seek out wisdom and reason: and to know the wickedness of the fool, and the error of the imprudent:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I have found a woman more bitter than death, who is the hunter’s snare, and her heart is a net, and her hands are bands. He that pleaseth God shall escape from her: but he that is a sinner, shall be caught by her.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Lo this have I found, said Ecclesiastes, weighing one thing after another, that I might find out the account,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Which yet my soul seeketh, and I have not found it. One man among a thousand I have found, a woman among them all I have not found.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Only this I have found, that God made man right, and he hath entangled himself with an infinity of questions. Who is as the wise man? and who hath known the resolution of the word?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The wisdom of a man shineth in his countenance, and the most mighty will change his face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I observe the mouth of the king, and the commandments of the oath of God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Be not hasty to depart from his face, and do not continue in an evil work: for he will do all that pleaseth him:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And his word is full of power: neither can any man say to him: Why dost thou so?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that keepeth the commandment, shall find no evil. The heart of a wise man understandeth time and answer.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is a time and opportunity for every business, and great affliction for man:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Because he is ignorant of things past, and things to come he cannot know by any messenger.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: It is not in man’s power to stop the spirit, neither hath he power in the day of death, neither is he suffered to rest when war is at hand, neither shall wickedness save the wicked.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All these things I have considered, and applied my heart to all the works that are done under the sun. Sometimes one man ruleth over another to his own hurt.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I saw the wicked buried: who also when they were yet living were in the holy place, and were praised in the city as men of just works: but this also is vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For because sentence is not speedily pronounced against the evil, the children of men commit evils without any fear.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But though a sinner do evil a hundred times, and by patience be borne withal, I know from thence that it shall be well with them that fear God, who dread his face.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But let it not be well with the wicked, neither let his days be prolonged, but as a shadow let them pass away that fear not the face of the Lord.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is also another vanity, which is done upon the earth. There are just men to whom evils happen, as though they had done the works of the wicked: and there are wicked men, who are as secure as though they had the deeds of the just: but this also I judge most vain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Therefore I commended mirth, because there was no good for a man under the sun, but to eat, and drink, and be merry, and that he should take nothing else with him of his labour in the days of his life, which God hath given him under the sun.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I applied my heart to know wisdom, and to understand the distraction that is upon earth: for there are some that day and night take no sleep with their eyes.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I understood that man can find no reason of all those works of God that are done under the sun: and the more he shall labour to seek, so much the less shall he find: yea, though the wise man shall say, that he knoweth it, he shall not be able to find it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: All these things have I considered in my heart, that I might carefully understand them: there are just men and wise men, and their works are in the hand of God: and yet man knoweth not whether he be worthy of love, or hatred:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: But all things are kept uncertain for the time to come, because all things equally happen to the just and to the wicked, to the good and to the evil, to the clean and to the unclean, to him that offereth victims, and to him that despiseth sacrifices. As the good is, so also is the sinner: as the perjured, so he also that sweareth truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This is a very great evil among all things that are done under the sun, that the same things happen to all men: whereby also the hearts of the children of men are filled with evil, and with contempt while they live, and afterwards they shall be brought down to hell.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is no man that liveth always, or that hopeth for this: a living dog is better than a dead lion.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For the living know that they shall die, but the dead know nothing more, neither have they a reward any more: for the memory of them is forgotten.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Their love also, and their hatred, and their envy are all perished, neither have they any part in this world, and in the work that is done under the sun.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Go then, and eat thy bread with joy, and drink thy wine with gladness: because thy works please God.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: At all times let thy garments be white, and let not oil depart from thy head.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Live joyfully with the wife whom thou lovest, all the days of thy unsteady life, which are given to thee under the sun, all the time of thy vanity: for this is thy portion in life, and in thy labour wherewith thou labourest under the sun.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Whatsoever thy hand is able to do, do it earnestly: for neither work, nor reason, nor wisdom, nor knowledge shall be in hell, whither thou art hastening.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I turned me to another thing, and I saw that under the sun, the race is not to the swift, nor the battle to the strong, nor bread to the wise, nor riches to the learned, nor favour to the skilful: but time and chance in all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Man knoweth not his own end: but as fishes are taken with the hook, and as birds are caught with the snare, so men are taken in the evil time, when it shall suddenly come upon them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: This wisdom also I have seen under the sun, and it seemed to me to be very great:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A little city, and few men in it: there came against it a great king, and invested it, and built bulwarks round about it, and the siege was perfect.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Now there was found in it a man poor and wise, and he delivered the city by his wisdom, and no man afterward remembered that poor man.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And I said that wisdom is better than strength: how then is the wisdom of the poor man slighted, and his words not heard?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The words of the wise are heard in silence, more than the cry of a prince among fools.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Better is wisdom, than weapons of war: and he that shall offend in one, shall lose many good things.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Dying flies spoil the sweetness of the ointment. Wisdom and glory is more precious than a small and shortlived folly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The heart of a wise man is in his right hand, and the heart of a fool is in his left hand.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Yea, and the fool when he walketh in the way, whereas he himself is a fool, esteemeth all men fools.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If the spirit of him that hath power, ascend upon thee, leave not thy place: because care will make the greatest sins to cease.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, as it were by an error proceeding from the face of the prince:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool set in high dignity, and the rich sitting beneath.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: I have seen servants upon horses: and princes walking on the ground as servants.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that diggeth a pit, shall fall into it: and he that breaketh a hedge, a serpent shall bite him.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that removeth stones, shall be hurt by them: and he that cutteth trees, shall be wounded by them.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If the iron be blunt, and be not as before, but be made blunt, with much labour it shall be sharpened: and after industry shall follow wisdom.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If a serpent bite in silence, he is nothing better that backbiteth secretly.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The words of the mouth of a wise man are grace: but the lips of a fool shall throw him down headlong.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The beginning of his words is folly, and the end of his talk is a mischievous error.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: A fool multiplieth words. A man cannot tell what hath been before him: and what shall be after him, who can tell him?

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The labour of fools shall afflict them that know not how to go to the city.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Woe to thee, O land, when thy king is a child, and when the princes eat in the morning.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Blessed is the land, whose king is noble, and whose princes eat in due season for refreshment, and not for riotousness.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: By slothfulness a building shall be brought down, and through the weakness of hands, the house shall drop through.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: For laughter they make bread, and wine that the living may feast: and all things obey money.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Detract not the king, no not in thy thought; and speak not evil of the rich man in thy private chamber: because even the birds of the air will carry thy voice, and he that hath wings will tell what thou hast said.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Cast thy bread upon the running waters: for after a long time thou shalt find it again.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Give a portion to seven, and also to eight: for thou knowest not what evil shall be upon the earth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If the clouds be full, they will pour out rain upon the earth. If the tree fall to the south, or to the north, in what place soever it shall fall, there shall it be.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He that observeth the wind, shall not sow: and he that considereth the clouds, shall never reap.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: As thou knowest not what is the way of the spirit, nor how the bones are joined together in the womb of her that is with child: so thou knowest not the works of God, who is the maker of all.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: In the morning sow thy seed, and in the evening let not thy hand cease: for thou knowest not which may rather spring up, this or that: and if both together, it shall be the better.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The light is sweet, and it is delightful for the eyes to see the sun.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: If a man live many years, and have rejoiced in them all, he must remember the darksome time, and the many days: which when they shall come, the things past shall be accused of vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Rejoice therefore, O young man, in thy youth, and let thy heart be in that which is good in the days of thy youth, and walk in the ways of thy heart, and in the sight of thy eyes: and know that for all these God will bring thee into judgment.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remove anger from thy heart, and put away evil from thy flesh. For youth and pleasure are vain.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Remember thy Creator in the days of thy youth, before the time of affliction come, and the years draw nigh of which thou shalt say: They please me not:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before the sun, and the light, and the moon, and the stars be darkened, and the clouds return after the rain:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: When the keepers of the house shall tremble, and the strong men shall stagger, and the grinders shall be idle in a small number, and they that look through the holes shall be darkened:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall shut the doors in the street, when the grinder’s voice shall be low, and they shall rise up at the voice of the bird, and all the daughters of music shall grow deaf.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And they shall fear high things, and they shall be afraid in the way, the almond tree shall flourish, the locust shall be made fat, and the caper tree shall be destroyed: because man shall go into the house of his eternity, and the mourners shall go round about in the street.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Before the silver cord be broken, and the golden fillet shrink back, and the pitcher be crushed at the fountain, and the wheel be broken upon the cistern,

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And the dust return into its earth, from whence it was, and the spirit return to God, who gave it.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Vanity of vanities, said Ecclesiastes, and all things are vanity.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And whereas Ecclesiastes was very wise, he taught the people, and declared the things that he had done: and seeking out, he set forth many parables.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: He sought profitable words, and wrote words most right, and full of truth.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: The words of the wise are as goads, and as nails deeply fastened in, which by the counsel of masters are given from one shepherd.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: More than these, my son, require not. Of making many books there is no end: and much study is an affliction of the flesh.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: Let us all hear together the conclusion of the discourse. Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this is all man:

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner

text: And all things that are done, God will bring into judgment for every error, whether it be good or evil.

source.translator: English College at Douay/Rheims; revised by Bishop Richard Challoner
